{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks whether bacteria release extracellular vesicles. Multiple documents from the MEDCORP source directly support this. For instance, Document [0] mentions that outer membrane vesicles (OMVs), which are derived from the outer membrane of gram-negative bacteria like Neisseria meningitides, are used as vaccines and are a form of extracellular vesicles. Document [10] explicitly states that outer-membrane vesicles (OMVs) are vesicles that bud out of the outer membrane of bacteria and are commonly produced by gram-negative bacteria. Document [2] notes that proteomic studies on bacterial EVs show selective loading of proteins, indicating their biological activity. Document [5] states that pathogens, including bacteria, use EVs to evade the immune system and deliver virulence factors. These references collectively confirm that bacteria do release extracellular vesicles. The UMLS documents do not provide direct evidence on this point and focus more on vaccines, antimicrobials, and classifications, lacking specific information about bacterial release of extracellular vesicles. Therefore, the MEDCORP documents provide clear and direct evidence that bacteria release extracellular vesicles.", "answer_choice": "A"}
